{{Short description|Application of quantum theory mathematics to cognitive phenomena}}
{{Distinguish|Quantum mind}}

'''Quantum cognition''' uses the mathematical formalism of quantum probability theory to model psychology phenomena when classical [[probability theory]] fails.<ref name=PothosBusemeyer2022Review/> The field focuses on modeling phenomena in [[cognitive science]] that have resisted traditional techniques or where traditional models seem to have reached a barrier (e.g., human memory),<ref name=Bruza2009>{{cite journal |last1=Bruza |first1=P. |last2=Kitto |first2=K. |last3=Nelson |first3=D. |last4=McEvoy |first4=C. |year=2009 |title=Is there something quantum-like about the human mental lexicon? |journal=Journal of Mathematical Psychology |volume=53 |issue=5 |pages=362–377 |doi=10.1016/j.jmp.2009.04.004 |pmid=20224806 |pmc=2834425 }}</ref> and modeling preferences in [[decision theory]] that seem paradoxical from a traditional rational point of view (e.g., preference reversals).<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Lambert Mogiliansky |first1=A. |last2=Zamir |first2=S. |last3=Zwirn |first3=H. |year=2009 |title=Type indeterminacy: A model of the KT (Kahneman–Tversky)-man |journal=Journal of Mathematical Psychology |volume=53 |issue=5 |pages=349–361 |doi=10.1016/j.jmp.2009.01.001 |arxiv=physics/0604166 |s2cid=15463046 }}</ref> Since the use of a quantum-theoretic framework is for modeling purposes, the identification of quantum structures in cognitive phenomena does not presuppose the existence of microscopic quantum processes in the human brain.<ref name=deBarrosSuppes2009>{{cite journal |last1=de Barros |first1=J. A. |last2=Suppes |first2=P. |year=2009 |title=Quantum mechanics, interference, and the brain |journal=Journal of Mathematical Psychology |volume=53 |issue=5 |pages=306–313 |doi=10.1016/j.jmp.2009.03.005 }}</ref><ref name=QuantumLike>{{cite journal |last=Khrennikov |first=A. |year=2008 |title=The Quantum-Like Brain on the Cognitive and Subcognitive Time Scales |journal=Journal of Consciousness Studies |volume=15 |issue=7 |pages=39–77 |issn=1355-8250 }}</ref>

Quantum cognition can be applied to model cognitive phenomena such as [[Information processing (psychology)|information processing]]<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Pothos |first1=E. M. |last2=Busemeyer |first2=J. R. |year=2013 |title=Can quantum probability provide a new direction for cognitive modeling |journal=Behavioral and Brain Sciences |volume=36 |issue=3 |pages=255–274 |doi=10.1017/S0140525X12001525 |pmid=23673021 |s2cid=53130527 }}</ref> by the [[human brain]], [[language]], [[decision making]],<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Aerts |first1=D. |last2=Aerts |first2=S. |year=1994 |title=Applications of quantum statistics in psychological studies of decision processes |journal=Foundations of Science |volume=1 |pages=85–97 |doi=10.1007/BF00208726 }}</ref> [[human memory]], [[concept]]s and conceptual reasoning, human [[judgment]], and [[perception]].<ref name=Khrennikov2010/><ref name=BusemeyerBruza2012/><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Wang |first1=Z. |last2=Busemeyer |first2=J. R. |last3=Atmanspacher |first3=H. |last4=Pothos |first4=E. M. |year=2013 |title=The potential of using quantum theory to build models of cognition |journal=Topics in Cognitive Science |volume=5 |issue=4 |pages=672–688 |doi=10.1111/tops.12043 |pmid=24027215 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

== Challenges for classical probability theory ==
Classical probability theory is a rational approach to inference which does not easily explain some observations of human inference in psychology.
Some cases where quantum probability theory has advantages include the [[conjunction fallacy]], the [[Representativeness_heuristic#Disjunction_fallacy | disjunction fallacy]], the failures of the [[sure-thing principle]], and [[Response_bias#Question_order_bias | question-order bias]] in judgement.<ref name=PothosBusemeyer2022Review>{{Cite journal |last1=Pothos |first1=Emmanuel M. |last2=Busemeyer |first2=Jerome R. |date=2022-01-04 |title=Quantum Cognition |url=https://www.annualreviews.org/doi/10.1146/annurev-psych-033020-123501 |journal=Annual Review of Psychology |language=en |volume=73 |issue=1 |pages=749–778 |doi=10.1146/annurev-psych-033020-123501 |pmid=34546804 |issn=0066-4308|url-access=subscription }}</ref>{{rp|752}}

=== Conjunction fallacy ===
If participants in a psychology experiment are told about "Linda", described as looking like a feminist but not like a bank teller, then asked to rank the probability, <math>P</math> that Linda is feminist, a bank teller or a feminist and a bank teller, they respond with values that indicate:
<math display="block">P(\text{feminist}) > P(\text{feminist}\ \&\  \text{bank teller}) > P(\text{bank teller}) </math>
Rational classical probability theory makes the incorrect prediction: it expects humans to rank the conjunction less probable than the bank teller option. Many variations of this experiment demonstrate that the fallacy represents human cognition in this case and not an artifact of one presentation.<ref name=PothosBusemeyer2022Review/>{{rp|753}}

Quantum cognition models this probability-estimation scenario with quantum probability theory which always ranks sequential probability, <math>P(\text{feminist}\ \&\ \text{bank teller})</math>, greater than the direct probability, <math>P(\text{bank teller})</math>. The idea is that a person's understanding of "bank teller" is affected by the context of the question involving "feminist".<ref name=PothosBusemeyer2022Review/>{{rp|753}} The two questions are "incompatible": to treat them with classical theory would require separate reasoning steps.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Pothos |first1=Emmanuel M. |last2=Busemeyer |first2=Jerome R. |last3=Shiffrin |first3=Richard M. |last4=Yearsley |first4=James M. |date=July 2017 |title=The rational status of quantum cognition. |url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/xge0000312 |journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: General |language=en |volume=146 |issue=7 |pages=968–987 |doi=10.1037/xge0000312 |pmid=28447840 |issn=1939-2222}}</ref>

==Main subjects of research ==

===Quantum-like models of information processing ===
The quantum cognition concept is based on the observation that various cognitive phenomena are more adequately described by quantum probability theory than by the classical probability theory (see examples below). Thus, the quantum formalism is considered an operational formalism that describes non-classical processing of probabilistic data. 

Here, contextuality is the key word (see the monograph of Khrennikov for detailed representation of this viewpoint).<ref name=Khrennikov2010>{{cite book |last=Khrennikov |first=A. |title=Ubiquitous Quantum Structure: from Psychology to Finances |publisher=Springer |year=2010 |isbn=978-3-642-42495-3 }}</ref> Quantum mechanics is fundamentally contextual.<ref>{{cite book |last=Khrennikov |first=A. |title=Contextual Approach to Quantum Formalism |series=Fundamental Theories of Physics |volume=160 |publisher=Springer |year=2009 |isbn=978-1-4020-9592-4 }}</ref> Quantum systems do not have objective properties which can be defined independently of measurement context. As has been pointed out by [[Niels Bohr]], the whole experimental arrangement must be taken into account. Contextuality implies existence of incompatible mental variables, violation of the classical law of total probability, and constructive or destructive interference effects. Thus, the quantum cognition approach can be considered an attempt to formalize contextuality of mental processes, by using the mathematical apparatus of quantum mechanics.

===Decision making===
Suppose a person is given an opportunity to play two rounds of the following gamble: a coin toss will determine whether the subject wins $200 or loses $100. Suppose the subject has decided to play the first round, and does so. Some subjects are then given the result (win or lose) of the first round, while other subjects are not yet given any information about the results. The experimenter then asks whether the subject wishes to play the second round. Performing this experiment with real subjects gives the following results:

# When subjects believe they won the first round, the majority of subjects choose to play again on the second round.
# When subjects believe they lost the first round, the majority of subjects choose to play again on the second round.

Given these two separate choices, according to the ''sure thing'' principle of rational decision theory, they should also play the second round even if they don't know or think about the outcome of the first round.<ref>{{cite book |last=Savage |first=L. J. |author-link=Leonard Jimmie Savage |year=1954 |title=The Foundations of Statistics |publisher=John Wiley & Sons }}</ref> 
But, experimentally, when subjects are not told the results of the first round, the majority of them decline to play a second round.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Tversky |first1=A. |author-link=Amos Tversky |last2=Shafir |first2=E. |author-link2=Eldar Shafir |year=1992 |title=The disjunction effect in choice under uncertainty |journal=Psychological Science |volume=3 |issue=5 |pages=305–309 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-9280.1992.tb00678.x |s2cid=144374616 }}</ref>
This finding violates the law of total probability, yet it can be explained as a [[quantum interference]] effect in a manner similar to the explanation for the results from [[double-slit experiment]] in quantum physics.<ref name=BusemeyerBruza2012>{{cite book |last1=Busemeyer |first1=J. |last2=Bruza |first2=P. |year=2012 |title=Quantum Models of Cognition and Decision |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=978-1-107-01199-1 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Pothos |first1=E. M. |last2=Busemeyer |first2=J. R. |year=2009 |title=A quantum probability explanation for violations of 'rational' decision theory |journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society |series=B: Biological Sciences |volume=276 |issue=1665 |pages=2171–2178 |doi=10.1098/rspb.2009.0121 |pmid=19324743 |pmc=2677606 }}</ref><ref name=YukalovSornette2009>{{cite journal |last1=Yukalov |first1=V. I. |last2=Sornette |first2=D. |title=Decision theory with prospect interference and entanglement |journal=Theory and Decision |date=21 February 2010 |volume=70 |issue=3 |pages=283–328 |doi=10.1007/s11238-010-9202-y |url=https://doc.rero.ch/record/315777/files/11238_2010_Article_9202.pdf |hdl=20.500.11850/29070 |s2cid=15377072 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> Similar violations of the sure-thing principle are seen in empirical studies of the [[Prisoner's Dilemma]] and have likewise been modeled in terms of quantum interference.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Musser |first1=George |title=A New Enlightenment |journal=Scientific American |date=16 October 2012 |volume=307 |issue=5 |pages=76–81 |doi=10.1038/scientificamerican1112-76 }}</ref>

The above deviations from classical rational expectations in agents’ decisions under uncertainty produce well known paradoxes in behavioral economics, that is, the [[Allais paradox|Allais]], [[Ellsberg paradox|Ellsberg]] and Machina paradoxes.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Allais |first=M. |author-link=Maurice Allais |year=1953 |title=Le comportement de l'homme rationnel devant le risque: Critique des postulats et axiomes de l'ecole Americaine |journal=[[Econometrica]] |volume=21 |issue=4 |pages=503–546 |doi=10.2307/1907921 |jstor=1907921 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last=Ellsberg |first=D. |author-link=Daniel Ellsberg |year=1961 |title=Risk, ambiguity, and the Savage axioms |journal=[[Quarterly Journal of Economics]] |volume=75 |issue=4 |pages=643–669 |doi=10.2307/1884324 |jstor=1884324 |url=http://www.dklevine.com/archive/refs47605.pdf }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last=Machina |first=M. J. |year=2009 |title=Risk, Ambiguity, and the Rank-Dependence Axioms |journal=[[American Economic Review]] |volume=99 |issue=1 |pages=385–392 |doi=10.1257/aer.99.1.385 }}</ref> These deviations can be explained if one assumes that the overall conceptual landscape influences the subject's choice in a neither predictable nor controllable way. A decision process is thus an intrinsically contextual process, hence it cannot be modeled in a single Kolmogorovian probability space, which justifies the employment of quantum probability models in decision theory. More explicitly, the paradoxical situations above can be represented in a unified Hilbert space formalism where human behavior under uncertainty is explained in terms of genuine quantum aspects, namely, superposition, interference, contextuality and incompatibility.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Aerts |first1=D. |last2=Sozzo |first2=S. |last3=Tapia |first3=J. |year=2012 |chapter=A quantum model for the Ellsberg and Machina paradoxes |title=Quantum Interaction 2012 |editor-last=Busemeyer |editor-first=J. |editor2-last=Dubois |editor2-first=F. |editor3-last=Lambert-Mogilansky |editor3-first=A. |pages=48–59 |series=LNCS |volume=7620 |publisher=Springer |location=Berlin }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Aerts |first1=D. |last2=Sozzo |first2=S. |last3=Tapia |first3=J. |year=2014 |title=Identifying quantum structures in the Ellsberg paradox |arxiv=1302.3850 |journal=International Journal of Theoretical Physics |volume=53 |issue=10 |pages=3666–3682 |doi=10.1007/s10773-014-2086-9 |bibcode=2014IJTP...53.3666A |s2cid=119158347 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last=La Mura |first=P. |year=2009 |title=Projective expected utility |journal=Journal of Mathematical Psychology |volume=53 |issue=5 |pages=408–414 |doi=10.1016/j.jmp.2009.02.001 |arxiv=0802.3300 |s2cid=12099816 }}</ref><ref name=YukalovSornette2009/>

Considering automated decision making, quantum [[decision trees]] have different structure compared to classical decision trees. Data can be analyzed to see if a quantum [[decision tree model]] fits the data better.<ref>{{cite conference |last=Kak |first=S. |year=2017 |title=Incomplete Information and Quantum Decision Trees |conference=IEEE International Conference on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics. Banff, Canada, October |doi=10.1109/SMC.2017.8122615 }}</ref>

===Human probability judgments===
Quantum probability provides a new way to explain human probability judgment errors including the conjunction and disjunction errors.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Tversky |first1=A. |last2=Kahneman |first2=D. |year=1983 |title=Extensional versus intuitive reasoning: The conjunction fallacy in probability judgment |journal=Psychological Review |volume=90 |issue=4 |pages=293–315 |doi=10.1037/0033-295X.90.4.293 }}</ref> A conjunction error occurs when a person judges the probability of a likely event L ''and'' an unlikely event U to be greater than the unlikely event U; a disjunction error occurs when a person judges the probability of a likely event L to be greater than the probability of the likely event L ''or'' an unlikely event U. Quantum probability theory is a generalization of [[Bayesian probability]] theory because it is based on a set of [[John von Neumann|von Neumann]] axioms that relax some of the classic [[Kolmogorov]] axioms.<ref name=Bond2018>{{Cite journal |last1=Bond |first1=Rachael L. |last2=He |first2=Yang-Hui |last3=Ormerod |first3=Thomas C. |date=2018 |title=A quantum framework for likelihood ratios |journal=[[International Journal of Quantum Information]] |volume=16 |issue=1 |pages=1850002 |arxiv=1508.00936 |doi=10.1142/s0219749918500028 |issn=0219-7499 |bibcode=2018IJQI...1650002B |s2cid=85523100 }}</ref> The quantum model introduces a new fundamental concept to cognition—the compatibility versus incompatibility of questions and the effect this can have on the sequential order of judgments. Quantum probability provides a simple account of conjunction and disjunction errors as well as many other findings such as order effects on probability judgments.<ref name=Aerts2009>{{cite journal |last=Aerts |first=D. |year=2009 |title=Quantum structure in cognition |journal=Journal of Mathematical Psychology |volume=53 |issue=5 |pages=314–348 |doi=10.1016/j.jmp.2009.04.005 |arxiv=0805.3850 |s2cid=14436506 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Busemeyer |first1=J. R. |last2=Pothos |first2=E. |last3=Franco |first3=R. |last4=Trueblood |first4=J. S. |year=2011 |title=A quantum theoretical explanation for probability judgment 'errors' |journal=Psychological Review |volume=118 |issue=2 |pages=193–218 |doi=10.1037/a0022542 |pmid=21480739 |url=https://openaccess.city.ac.uk/id/eprint/1977/1/QP4_1.pdf }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Trueblood |first1=J. S. |last2=Busemeyer |first2=J. R. |year=2011 |title=A quantum probability account of order effects in inference |journal=Cognitive Science |volume=35 |issue=8 |pages=1518–1552 |doi=10.1111/j.1551-6709.2011.01197.x |pmid=21951058 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

The liar paradox - The contextual influence of a human subject on the truth behavior of a cognitive entity is explicitly exhibited by the so-called [[liar paradox]], that is, the truth value of a sentence like "this sentence is false". One can show that the true-false state of this paradox is represented in a complex Hilbert space, while the typical oscillations between true and false are dynamically described by the Schrödinger equation.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Aerts |first1=D. |last2=Broekaert |first2=J. |author-link3=Sonja Smets |last3=Smets |first3=S. |year=1999 |title=The liar paradox in a quantum mechanical perspective |journal=Foundations of Science |volume=4 |issue=2 |pages=115–132 |doi=10.1023/A:1009610326206 |s2cid=119404170 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Aerts |first1=D. |last2=Aerts |first2=S. |last3=Broekaert |first3=J. |last4=Gabora |first4=L. |year=2000 |title=The violation of Bell inequalities in the macroworld |journal=Foundations of Physics |volume=30 |issue=9 |pages=1387–1414 |doi=10.1023/A:1026449716544 |arxiv=quant-ph/0007044 |bibcode=2000quant.ph..7044A |s2cid=3262876 }}</ref>

===Knowledge representation===
Concepts are basic cognitive phenomena, which provide the content for inference, explanation, and language understanding. [[Cognitive psychology]] has researched different approaches for [[Concept learning|understanding concepts]] including exemplars, prototypes, and [[neural networks]], and different fundamental problems have been identified, such as the experimentally tested non classical behavior for the conjunction and disjunction of concepts, more specifically the Pet-Fish problem or guppy effect,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Osherson |first1=D. N. |last2=Smith |first2=E. E. |year=1981 |title=On the adequacy of prototype theory as a theory of concepts |journal=Cognition |volume=9 |issue=1 |pages=35–58 |doi=10.1016/0010-0277(81)90013-5 |pmid=7196818 |s2cid=10482356 }}</ref> and the overextension and underextension of typicality and membership weight for conjunction and disjunction.<ref name=Hampton1988a>{{cite journal |ref=Hampton1988a |last=Hampton |first=J. A. |year=1988 |title=Overextension of conjunctive concepts: Evidence for a unitary model for concept typicality and class inclusion |journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition |volume=14 |issue=1 |pages=12–32 |doi=10.1037/0278-7393.14.1.12 }}</ref><ref name=Hampton1988b>{{cite journal |ref=Hampton1988b |last=Hampton |first=J. A. |year=1988 |title=Disjunction of natural concepts |journal=Memory & Cognition |volume=16 |issue=6 |pages=579–591 |doi=10.3758/BF03197059 |pmid=3193889 |doi-access=free }}</ref> By and large, quantum cognition has drawn on quantum theory in three ways to model concepts.
# Exploit the contextuality of quantum theory to account for the contextuality of concepts in cognition and language and the phenomenon of emergent properties when concepts combine<ref name="deBarrosSuppes2009"/><ref name=AertsGabora2005a>{{cite journal |last1=Aerts |first1=D. |last2=Gabora |first2=L. |year=2005 |title=A state-context-property model of concepts and their combinations I: The structure of the sets of contexts and properties |journal=Kybernetes |volume=34 |issue=1&2 |pages=167–191 |doi=10.1108/03684920510575799 |arxiv=quant-ph/0402207 |s2cid=15124657 }}</ref><ref name=AertsGabora2005b>{{cite journal |last1=Aerts |first1=D. |last2=Gabora |first2=L. |year=2005 |title=A state-context-property model of concepts and their combinations II: A Hilbert space representation |journal=Kybernetes |volume=34 |issue=1&2 |pages=192–221 |doi=10.1108/03684920510575807 |arxiv=quant-ph/0402205 |s2cid=13988880 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Gabora |first1=L. |last2=Aerts |first2=D. |year=2002 |title=Contextualizing concepts using a mathematical generalization of the quantum formalism |journal=[[Journal of Experimental and Theoretical Artificial Intelligence]] |volume=14 |issue=4 |pages=327–358 |doi=10.1080/09528130210162253 |arxiv=quant-ph/0205161 |s2cid=10643452 }}</ref><ref name=WiddowsPeters2003>{{cite conference |last1=Widdows |first1=D. |last2=Peters |first2=S. |year=2003 |title=Word Vectors and Quantum Logic: Experiments with negation and disjunction |conference=Eighth Mathematics of Language Conference |pages=141–154 }}</ref>
# Use quantum [[Quantum entanglement|entanglement]] to model the semantics of concept combinations in a non-decompositional way, and to account for the emergent properties/associates/inferences in relation to concept combinations<ref>{{cite book |last1=Bruza |first1=P. D. |last2=Cole |first2=R. J. |year=2005 |chapter=Quantum logic of semantic space: An exploratory investigation of context effects in practical reasoning |editor-link=Sergei N. Artemov |editor-first=S. |editor-last=Artemov |editor2-first=H. |editor2-last=Barringer |editor3-link=A. S. d'Avila Garcez |editor3-first=A. S. |editor3-last=d'Avila Garcez |editor4-first=L. C. |editor4-last=Lamb |editor5-first=J. |editor5-last=Woods |title=We Will Show Them: Essays in Honour of Dov Gabbay |publisher=College Publications |isbn=1-904987-11-7 }}</ref>
#Use [[quantum superposition]] to account for the emergence of a new concept when concepts are combined, and as a consequence put forward an explanatory model for the Pet-Fish problem situation, and the overextension and underextension of membership weights for the conjunction and disjunction of concepts.<ref name=Aerts2009/><ref name=AertsGabora2005a/><ref name=AertsGabora2005b/>

The large amount of data collected by Hampton<ref name=Hampton1988a/><ref name=Hampton1988b/> on the combination of two concepts can be modeled in a specific quantum-theoretic framework in Fock space where the observed deviations from classical set (fuzzy set) theory, the above-mentioned over- and under- extension of membership weights, are explained in terms of contextual interactions, superposition, interference, entanglement and emergence.<ref name=Aerts2009/><ref>{{cite journal |last=Aerts |first=D. |year=2009 |title=Quantum particles as conceptual entities: A possible explanatory framework for quantum theory |journal=Foundations of Science |volume=14 |issue=4 |pages=361–411 |doi= 10.1007/s10699-009-9166-y |arxiv=1004.2530 |s2cid=119209842 }}</ref><ref name="Aerts2013">{{cite journal |last1=Aerts |first1=D. |last2=Broekaert |first2=J. |last3=Gabora |first3=L. |last4=Sozzo |first4=S. |year=2013 |title=Quantum structure and human thought |journal=Behavioral and Brain Sciences |volume=36 |issue=3 |pages=274–276 |doi=10.1017/S0140525X12002841 |pmid=23673022 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Aerts|first1=Diederik|last2=Gabora|first2=Liane|last3=Sozzo|first3=Sandro|title=Concepts and Their Dynamics: A Quantum-Theoretic Modeling of Human Thought|journal=Topics in Cognitive Science|date=September 2013|volume=5|issue=4|pages=737–772|doi=10.1111/tops.12042|pmid=24039114|arxiv=1206.1069|s2cid=6300002}}</ref> And, more, a cognitive test on a specific concept combination has been performed which directly reveals, through the violation of Bell's inequalities, quantum entanglement between the component concepts.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Aerts |first1=D. |last2=Sozzo |first2=S. |year=2012 |chapter=Quantum structures in cognition: Why and how concepts are entangled |title=Quantum Interaction 2011 |editor-last=Song |editor-first=D. |editor2-last=Melucci |editor2-first=M. |editor3-last=Frommholz |editor3-first=I. |pages=116–127 |series=LNCS |volume=7052 |publisher=Springer |location=Berlin |isbn=978-3-642-24970-9 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Aerts |first1=D. |last2=Sozzo |first2=S. |year=2014 |title=Quantum entanglement in concept combinations |journal=International Journal of Theoretical Physics |volume=53 |issue=10 |pages=3587–3603 |doi=10.1007/s10773-013-1946-z |arxiv=1302.3831 |bibcode=2014IJTP...53.3587A |s2cid=17064563 }}</ref>

===Semantic analysis and information retrieval===
The research in (iv) had a deep impact on the understanding and initial development of a formalism to obtain semantic information when dealing with concepts, their combinations and variable contexts in a corpus of unstructured documents. This conundrum of [[natural language processing]] (NLP) and [[information retrieval]] (IR) on the web – and data bases in general – can be addressed using the mathematical formalism of quantum theory. As basic steps, (a) K. Van Rijsbergen introduced a quantum structure approach to IR,<ref>{{cite book |last=Van Rijsbergen |first=K. |year=2004 |title=The Geometry of Information Retrieval |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=0-521-83805-3 }}</ref> (b) Widdows and Peters utilised a quantum logical negation for a concrete search system,<ref name=WiddowsPeters2003/><ref>{{cite book |last=Widdows |first=D. |year=2006 |title=Geometry and meaning |publisher=CSLI Publications |isbn=1-57586-448-7 }}</ref> and Aerts and Czachor identified quantum structure in semantic space theories, such as [[latent semantic analysis]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Aerts |first1=D. |last2=Czachor |first2=M. |year=2004 |arxiv=quant-ph/0309022 |title=Quantum aspects of semantic analysis and symbolic artificial intelligence |journal=Journal of Physics A |volume=37 |issue=12 |pages=L123–L132 |doi=10.1088/0305-4470/37/12/L01 |s2cid=16701954 }}</ref> Since then, the employment of techniques and procedures induced from the mathematical formalisms of quantum theory – Hilbert space, quantum logic and probability, non-commutative algebras, etc. – in fields such as IR and NLP, has produced significant results.<ref>{{cite web |title=Parserless Extraction; Using a Multidimensional Transient State Vector Machine |first=Michael |last=Sorah |url=https://www.rosoka.com/sites/default/files/ParserlessExtraction.pdf |access-date=2017-04-03 |archive-date=2017-04-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170404131640/https://www.rosoka.com/sites/default/files/ParserlessExtraction.pdf |url-status=dead }}</ref>

==History==
Ideas for applying the formalisms of quantum theory to cognition first appeared in the 1990s by [[Diederik Aerts]] and his collaborators Jan Broekaert, [[Sonja Smets]] and Liane Gabora, by Harald Atmanspacher, Robert Bordley, and [[Andrei Khrennikov]]. A special issue on ''Quantum Cognition and Decision'' appeared in the ''[[Journal of Mathematical Psychology]]'' (2009, vol 53.), which planted a flag for the field. A few books related to quantum cognition have been published including those by Khrennikov (2004, 2010), Ivancivic and Ivancivic (2010), Busemeyer and Bruza (2012), E. Conte (2012). The first Quantum Interaction workshop was held at [[Stanford]] in 2007 organized by Peter Bruza, William Lawless, C. J. van Rijsbergen, and Don Sofge as part of the 2007 [[AAAI]] Spring Symposium Series. This was followed by workshops at [[Oxford]] in 2008, [[Saarbrücken]] in 2009, at the 2010 AAAI Fall Symposium Series held in [[Washington, D.C.]], 2011 in [[Aberdeen]], 2012 in [[Paris]], and 2013 in [[Leicester]]. Tutorials also were presented annually beginning in 2007 until 2013 at the annual meeting of the [[Cognitive Science Society]]. A ''Special Issue on Quantum models of Cognition'' appeared in 2013 in the journal ''[[Topics in Cognitive Science]]''.

==See also==

*{{annotated link|Bayesian approaches to brain function}}
*{{annotated link|Quantum Bayesianism}}
*{{annotated link|Quantum logic}}
{{Div col end}}

==Further reading==

*{{cite journal |last1=Busemeyer |first1=J. R. |last2=Wang |first2=Z. |year=2019 |title=Primer on quantum cognition |journal=Spanish Journal of Psychology |volume=22 |at=e53 |doi=10.1017/sjp.2019.51 |pmid=31868156 |s2cid=209446824 }}
*{{cite book |last=Conte |first=E. |year=2012 |title=Advances in application of quantum mechanics in neuroscience and psychology: a Clifford algebraic approach |publisher=Nova Science Publishers |isbn=978-1-61470-325-9 }}
*{{cite book |last1=Ivancevic |first1=V. |last2=Ivancevic |first2=T. |year=2010 |title=Quantum Neural Computation |publisher=Springer |isbn=978-90-481-3349-9 }}
* {{cite journal |last=Khrennikov |first=A. |year=2006 |title=Quantum-like brain: 'Interference of minds' |journal=Biosystems |volume=84 |issue=3 |pages=225–241 |doi=10.1016/j.biosystems.2005.11.005 |pmid=16427733 |arxiv=quant-ph/0205092 }}
* {{cite book |last=Khrennikov |first=A. |title=Information Dynamics in Cognitive, Psychological, Social, and Anomalous Phenomena |series=Fundamental Theories of Physics |volume=138 |publisher=Kluwer |year=2004 |isbn=1-4020-1868-1 }}
* {{cite journal |last1=Atmanspacher |first1=H. |last2=Römer |first2=H. |last3=Walach |first3=H. |year=2002 |title=Weak quantum theory: Complementarity and entanglement in physics and beyond |journal=Foundations of Physics |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=379–406 |doi=10.1023/A:1014809312397 |arxiv=quant-ph/0104109 |bibcode=2002FoPh...32..379A |s2cid=118583726 }}
